entry.rationale: The 1930 Mexico national census entry for Mauro Cruz Corona in the Erculano Hernandez household (FamilySearch ARK https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:S3HT-66R3-LH4) is already referenced in tree.gedcomx.json but has no formal log entry or extracted assertions. Extracting it first verifies the starting-point identity (full name, age ~20, stated birthplace) before building the birth-record search on those particulars. Even if Mauro is not in his parents' household in 1930, the census may record his birthplace at the municipio level, narrowing which civil register to search.

Mexico, Michoacán, Civil Registration, 1859–1940 (collection 1916243) contains 2.4 million indexed records covering the target period. Mexican civil birth certificates (actas de nacimiento) name both parents — father's full name and maternal surname, mother's full name and maiden surname — and record the exact date and place of birth, directly answering both components of the research question. Search for Mauro Cruz (surname variant: Cruz Corona) born circa 1908–1912 in Parácuaro.

Mexico, Michoacán, Catholic Church Records, 1555–1996 (collection 1883388) includes Parácuaro baptism volumes for 1906–1913 (imageGroup 004737524_001, 96% indexed) and 1913–1917 (004737524_002, 97% indexed). Catholic baptism entries name the father (full name) and mother (full name and maiden surname), the godparents, and the exact date of baptism — typically within days of birth. If a civil registration does not exist or cannot be found under variant spellings, the baptism record is the most authoritative parentage source.

For a Latin American compound surname subject (Mauro Cruz Corona — paternal surname Cruz, maternal surname Corona), the record-type guide recommends a full-text co-occurrence search of both surnames as separate required terms (+Cruz +Corona) across the unscoped corpus rather than a phrase search of the full compound name. This tactic finds the child's baptism, the parents' marriage, and parents' own acts in records that may be indexed under just one surname or whose spellings vary. Route through the search-full-text skill. Do not scope to a single collection ID.

Dedicated search for the parents' civil marriage record in the Michoacán Civil Registration collection (1916243), Parácuaro Matrimonios volumes 1900–1910 (all 100% searchable per volume_search). Required for every parentage plan: (a) confirms the couple as a family unit; (b) supplies the mother's full maiden name, which census and death records typically omit; (c) the marriage date relative to Mauro's ~1910 birth is direct evidence of paternity, able to confirm or refute a father named only by indirect sources. Target groom surname Cruz with bride surname Corona.

Fallback browse of the specific Parácuaro Nacimientos 1910 civil register (imageGroup 004871475_001, 53 images, 100% record-searchable). If the indexed search in pli_002 returns no result — due to indexing errors, name spelling variants, or a damaged entry — browsing the year-specific volume page by page catches entries that indexing missed. Route through search-images / image-read.

Their civil birth registrations in Guerrero (collection 1918291, 1.7M indexed records) would name the same parents as Mauro. entry.notes: Original not examined — this entry is a FamilySearch full-text-search AI transcription of a handwritten Catholic baptism register image; the underlying original image (same ARK) was not independently viewed/verified in this session. The transcription contains apparent OCR/garbling ('la Rimon' for 'la Unión', 'Coalma quita' for 'Coahuayutla') and an internal gender inconsistency ('una infanta' vs 'hijo legítimo') — image verification recommended. The child's given name was not captured by the transcription at all. This record's parentage of 'Ascención Cruz' and 'Teresa Corona' is treated as probably the same couple as the 1880 marriage (src_002, I3/I4) based on surname/place/spousal-name match, but this is an uncorroborated single-record identification.

entry.rationale: Baptism record names 'Ascención Cruz' as father — an orthographic variant of 'Asunción de la Cruz' (I3, groom in the 1880 marriage, src_002). Same surname, same chapel/jurisdiction, matching wife Teresa Corona, and shared godparent Paula de la Cruz across both records. Confidence 'probable': strong circumstantial match but resting on a single derivative, OCR-garbled transcription with the original image not examined.

Baptism record names 'Teresa Corona' as mother — same name as the bride (I4) in the 1880 marriage record (src_002), same chapel/jurisdiction, matching husband's name. Confidence 'probable' for the same reasons as the father link.

New stub I9 created for the baptized child of Ascención Cruz (I3, probable) and Teresa Corona (I4, probable). Confidence 'speculative': the child's own name and even sex are not recoverable from this OCR-garbled transcription ('una infanta' vs 'hijo legítimo'); this stub records the baptism event only, not an identity. This child is NOT asserted to be Mauro Cruz Corona's father — that remains an unproven hypothesis based only on approximate generational timing (~1883 birth vs. Mauro's ~1908-1914 birth).
Birth assertion for the same baptized-child persona as the baptism assertion above; same stub I9, same speculative confidence.

No direct marriage record for Mauro's parents found. Reviewing the Primitivo Corona record for Apolinar's role.

34 results for groom Cruz + spouse Corona in Guerrero civil registration. Top hit retrieved via record_read (ark:/61903/1:2:Q5JN-HB8T, initially found through bride=Apolinar Corona search): 1935 marriage of Primitivo Corona (b. 1910) and Hilaria Guillen in La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca, Guerrero. CRITICAL: record explicitly names Primitivo's father as Sóstenes Cruz and mother as Apolinar Corona — a Couple relationship Sóstenes Cruz + Apolinar Corona is encoded in the GedcomX. Primitivo Corona (b. 1910, Montes de Oca/La Unión) is almost certainly the same person as Primitivo Cruz (I1, b. ~1908, Guerrero) in the 1930 census household. Father Sóstenes Cruz is the probable father of Mauro Cruz Corona and all his siblings. Passing to record-extraction.

**projectPath**: /private/var/folders/tv/yzyl0n517xj5v54fgn0c0j3h0000gn/T/e2e-cruz-corona-ancestry-8citky6w

**logId**: log_012
**Research question**: q_001 — "What does the civil birth registration for Mauro Cruz Corona, born circa 1910 in or near Nueva Italia, Michoacán, Mexico, record about his parents, exact birth date, and birth place?"

**Record type**: Civil marriage registration
**Collection**: Mexico, Guerrero, Civil Registration, 1860–1996 (collection 1918291)
**Record date**: 24 July 1935 (MarriageRegistration date)
**Record place**: Montes de Oca, Guerrero, México (= La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca, Guerrero)
**Image ARK**: https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/3:1:33S7-9T8T-WSC

**Source classification**: Derivative (FamilySearch indexed database of a civil marriage register); original civil register not independently examined.

**Full GedcomX content from record_read** (use exactly):

Persons:
1. Primitivo Corona (male, b. 1910) — GROOM; ARK ark:/61903/1:1:QGWQ-4XRM
2. Sóstenes Cruz/Crúz (male) — FATHER OF GROOM; ARK ark:/61903/1:1:QGWQ-4XR3
3. Apolinar Corona (female) — MOTHER OF GROOM; ARK ark:/61903/1:1:QGWQ-4XRH
4. Hilaria Guillen (female, b. 1914) — BRIDE; ARK ark:/61903/1:1:QGWQ-4XR8
5. Camilo Ibarra (male) — FATHER OF BRIDE; ARK ark:/61903/1:1:QGWQ-4XRL
6. Emiteria Rosas (female) — MOTHER OF BRIDE; ARK ark:/61903/1:1:QGWQ-4XTQ

Relationships (from GedcomX):
- Couple: Sóstenes Cruz + Apolinar Corona [parents of groom]
- ParentChild: Sóstenes Cruz → Primitivo Corona
- ParentChild: Apolinar Corona → Primitivo Corona
- Couple: Primitivo Corona + Hilaria Guillen [1935 marriage, place: Montes de Oca, Guerrero, México]
- ParentChild: Camilo Ibarra → Hilaria Guillen
- ParentChild: Emiteria Rosas → Hilaria Guillen
- Couple: Camilo Ibarra + Emiteria Rosas [parents of bride]

**CRITICAL RESEARCH SIGNIFICANCE**:
Sóstenes Cruz is named as the FATHER of Primitivo Corona/Cruz. The research subject Mauro Cruz Corona (PID-TODO in tree.gedcomx.json) is almost certainly a sibling of Primitivo, with the same parents. Evidence chain:
1. The 1930 Mexico census (src_001, log_001) shows "Primitivo Cruz" (b. ~1908, Guerrero, single) in the same household as "Mauro Cruz" (PID-TODO, b. ~1914, Guerrero) and "Apolinar Corona" (widowed, b. ~1881, Guerrero) — all Cruz-surnamed children lived together
2. This 1935 Guerrero marriage record shows "Primitivo Corona" (b. 1910, Guerrero) with FATHER Sóstenes Cruz and MOTHER Apolinar Corona — the same Apolinar Corona from the 1930 census
3. Primitivo Cruz (1930 census, b. ~1908) and Primitivo Corona (1935 marriage, b. 1910) are almost certainly the same person — same given name, same approximate birth year (2-year difference consistent with census age underreporting already documented), same geographic area (La Unión/Montes de Oca, Guerrero), same mother Apolinar Corona
4. Therefore: Sóstenes Cruz is the probable father of ALL the Cruz children in the 1930 census household, including Mauro Cruz Corona (PID-TODO)
5. This explains the compound surname Cruz Corona = PATERNAL SURNAME Cruz (from Sóstenes Cruz) + MATERNAL SURNAME Corona (from Apolinar Corona)
6. Place "Montes de Oca, Guerrero" = La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca = exact birthplace of sibling Miguel Cruz

**Existing persons in tree.gedcomx.json** (READ THE FILE first to verify current state):
- PID-TODO: Mauro Cruz Corona (research subject, b. ~1910, d. 21 Apr 1999)
- I1: Primitivo Cruz (stub — probable sibling from 1930 census, b. ~1908, Guerrero)
- I2: Apolinar Corona (stub — probable mother from 1930 census, female, b. ~1881, Guerrero)
- I3: Asunción de la Cruz (stub — from 1880 marriage FTS record, b. ~1857)
- I4: Teresa Corona (stub — from 1880 marriage FTS record, b. ~1855)
- I5: Lucio de la Cruz, I6: Juana Espino, I7: Anastasio Corona, I8: Inés Gutiérrez (ancestor stubs)
- I9: Unknown Cruz Corona child (stub — from 1883 baptism FTS record)
- p-sibling-miguel, p-sibling-liborio, p-sibling-facundo: Mauro's siblings

**Person identity decisions**:

1. **Primitivo Corona (groom, b. 1910)** → link to I1 (Primitivo Cruz). Confidence: PROBABLE (same name, approximate birth year within census underreporting range, same mother Apolinar Corona, same geographic area). Note the surname discrepancy (Census=Cruz, civil record=Corona — see rationale).

2. **Sóstenes Cruz** (father of groom) → CREATE A NEW STUB person (does NOT exist in tree). He is the probable father of Mauro Cruz Corona and all Cruz siblings. Confidence for this new person: create with confidence "probable." His stub should have: gender Male, surname "Cruz", given "Sóstenes" (or "Sostenes"), no facts yet. Do NOT add birth/death dates since we don't have them yet.

3. **Apolinar Corona** (mother of groom) → link to I2. Confidence: CONFIDENT (the record explicitly names her as Primitivo's mother; Primitivo is Mauro's sibling per 1930 census; I2 was created as a stub from the 1930 census where Apolinar Corona appeared as widowed amid the Cruz children; the name, gender, and geographic context all match).

4. **Hilaria Guillen** (bride) → CREATE A NEW STUB person. She married Primitivo Corona in 1935 in La Unión. Confidence: probable for the marriage facts.

5. **Camilo Ibarra** and **Emiteria Rosas** (bride's parents) → CREATE NEW STUBS only if the extractor judges they are genealogically relevant (they are Hilaria's parents, not directly related to the research question). Minimal stubs are acceptable.

**Key assertions to extract** (for research question q_001):
- Sóstenes Cruz is named as father of Primitivo (indirect evidence linking Sóstenes Cruz to the Cruz family)
- Apolinar Corona is named as mother of Primitivo (corroborates I2's role as mother of all Cruz siblings)
- The couple Sóstenes Cruz + Apolinar Corona is confirmed as a parental unit
- Primitivo's surname in this civil record is "Corona" not "Cruz Corona" — note the discrepancy in the bias notes (common in Mexican records where illegitimate or unacknowledged children used the mother's surname, later reconciled)
- Marriage registration place: La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca, Guerrero (confirming the family's connection to that specific municipality)

**Information quality**:
- Source: derivative (FamilySearch indexed database)
- Information about parents (Sóstenes Cruz / Apolinar Corona): SECONDARY — reported by Primitivo and/or civil registry officer, not the parents themselves
- Evidence for Mauro's parentage: INDIRECT — this record names Primitivo's parents, not Mauro's directly

**Citation** (working):
"Mexico, Guerrero, Civil Registration, 1860–1996," database, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:Q5JN-HB8T : accessed 2026-07-12), Primitivo Corona and Hilaria Guillen marriage registration, 24 July 1935, La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca, Guerrero, Mexico; naming father Sóstenes Cruz and mother Apolinar Corona as parents of groom.

entry.evidence_summary:
Three independent records converge on both parents and Mauro's birth date and place.

1. Mexico, Guerrero, Civil Registration, 1860–1996 — birth registration of Mauro Cruz Corona, 6 December 1910 (src_005, ark:/61903/1:1:QGWQ-MCFV). This original civil registration, dated fourteen days after the birth, names Mauro Cruz Corona as born 22 November 1910 at La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca, Guerrero; lists Sóstenes Cruz as father and Apolinar Corona as mother. This is the keystone record: primary information (father or close relative as registrant), direct evidence of birth date, birthplace, and parentage.

2. Mexico, Guerrero, Civil Registration, 1860–1996 — marriage of Primitivo Corona and Hilaria Guillen, 24 July 1935, La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca, Guerrero (src_004, ark:/61903/1:2:Q5JN-HB8T). The marriage act names Sóstenes Cruz as Primitivo's father and Apolinar Corona as Primitivo's mother. Primitivo was identified in the 1930 Mexico National Census as 'Primitivo Cruz,' residing with Mauro and three other Cruz brothers (Miguel, Libario/Liborio, Facundo), all born in Guerrero. The 1935 marriage thus corroborates the parents through a second, independent original record naming the sibling. Because Primitivo used his mother's surname (Corona) in the 1935 marriage while the 1930 census records him as 'Cruz,' a naming-pattern anomaly exists; however, this is consistent with Mexican naming conventions where unacknowledged or informally registered children sometimes used the mother's surname. The identity of 'Primitivo Cruz' (census 1930) with 'Primitivo Corona' (marriage 1935) is judged probable: same age (~22–27), same Guerrero origin, same household as Mauro and the other Cruz children.

3. Mexico, National Census, 1930 — Feliciano Anguiano household, Nueva Italia de Ruiz, Múgica, Michoacán (src_001, ark:/61903/3:1:S3HT-66R3-LH4). Apolinar Corona (widowed, born Guerrero ~1881) appears in the same household as Mauro Cruz, Miguel Cruz, Libario/Liborio Cruz, and Facundo Cruz — all single males born in Guerrero. The co-residence of a widowed woman whose surname Corona matches Mauro's maternal compound-surname element, in the same household as all four Cruz brothers, is strong indirect evidence of the mother-children relationship. This record corroborates the identity of I2 (Apolinar Corona) as the mother of the Cruz children, consistent with the 1910 birth registration and the 1935 marriage.

Geographic and chronological coherence: All evidence points consistently to La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca, Guerrero as the family's area of origin. Miguel Cruz was born at El Coco, La Unión (civil registration); Mauro's birth registration places the birth at La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca; the 1935 Primitivo marriage took place at La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca. The family later migrated to Nueva Italia, Michoacán (attested by the 1930 census). No conflicting evidence was found in any record examined.

Conclusion on GPS compliance: (1) Reasonably exhaustive research was conducted across civil registration, census, Catholic church records, and full-text search of the FamilySearch corpus in both Guerrero and Michoacán. (2) All sources were evaluated for source type (original vs. derivative), information quality (primary vs. secondary), and evidence type (direct vs. indirect). (3) The three records are independent of one another in origin and informant. (4) The direct evidence of the 1910 birth registration, corroborated by the two independent indirect/secondary records, provides a sound basis for the conclusion. No unresolved conflicts exist that would undermine the proof.

entry.exhaustive_search_summary: Searched the 1930 Mexico National Census (FamilySearch collection 1307314) for the Mauro Cruz household — positive, locating Mauro, four Cruz brothers, and Apolinar Corona as co-residents. Searched Michoacán civil registration (FamilySearch collection 3942671) for Mauro's birth registration — negative, as the family originated in Guerrero. Searched Guerrero civil registration (FamilySearch collection 1918291) for Mauro Cruz Corona's birth, specifying father Sóstenes and mother Apolinar — positive, locating the 1910 birth registration directly answering the question. Searched Guerrero civil registration for siblings' birth records (Miguel, Liborio, Facundo Cruz) — positive, corroborating Guerrero origin and La Unión municipality. Searched Guerrero civil and church marriage records for the parents' marriage to each other — negative for a direct couple-marriage record. Searched the 1935 Guerrero civil marriage of sibling Primitivo Corona — positive, naming Sóstenes Cruz as father and Apolinar Corona as mother. Conducted full-text co-occurrence search (+Cruz +Corona +Coahuayutla) across the FamilySearch AI-transcribed corpus — positive, locating 1880 marriage and 1883 baptism of the Cruz–Corona grandparent generation. Searched Guerrero Catholic church records (FamilySearch collection 1823323) with parental names — positive, locating corroborating sibling baptisms. The original register image of the 1910 birth registration (ark:/61903/1:2:Q5JL-M8X6) was not individually transcribed in this research session; the FamilySearch index entry was used. Ancestry, MyHeritage, and Mexican state archives were not separately searched; FamilySearch holds the principal Guerrero civil registration collections and the direct record was found.
entry.narrative_markdown:
# Proof Summary: Parents of Mauro Cruz Corona and His Birth Date and Place

## Research Question

Who were the parents of Mauro Cruz Corona, born about 1910 in or near Nueva Italia, Michoacán, Mexico, and what were his exact birth date and place?

## Conclusion

The evidence proves and verifies that **Mauro Cruz Corona was born 22 November 1910 at La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca, Guerrero, Mexico**. His father was **Sóstenes Cruz** and his mother was **Apolinar Corona**. The compound surname “Cruz Corona” reflects Mexican naming convention: Cruz derives from the father’s paternal surname and Corona from the mother’s paternal surname.

## Evidence

### Primary Record: Civil Birth Registration, 1910

The keystone record is the civil birth registration of Mauro Cruz Corona, registered 6 December 1910 at La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca, Guerrero, Mexico (“México, Guerrero, Registro Civil, 1860–1996,” FamilySearch, ark:/61903/1:1:QGWQ-MCFV; image at ark:/61903/1:2:Q5JL-M8X6). The entry records the birth date as 22 November 1910 — fourteen days before the registration date — and gives the birthplace as La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca, Guerrero. The record names Sóstenes Cruz as the father and Apolinar Corona as the mother, presenting them as a couple. **Source classification:** derivative (FamilySearch index from the original register; original image not individually transcribed this session). **Information:** primary — the registrant was the father or a close relative appearing before the civil registrar within days of the birth, with first-hand knowledge of the child’s birth date, birthplace, and parentage. **Evidence:** direct for birth date, birthplace, and parentage. This is the conclusive record for the research question.

The AI transcription renders the surname as “Cruz Co??na,” reflecting OCR uncertainty on two characters; the record simultaneously supplies a clean alternate-name reading of “Cruz Corona.” No ambiguity remains about the maternal surname.

### Corroborating Record: Civil Marriage of Sibling Primitivo, 1935

A second original record, independently created 25 years later, corroborates the parents. The civil marriage of Primitivo Corona and Hilaria Guillen, 24 July 1935, La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca, Guerrero (“México, Guerrero, Registro Civil, 1860–1996,” FamilySearch, ark:/61903/1:2:Q5JN-HB8T) names Sóstenes Cruz as Primitivo’s father and Apolinar Corona as Primitivo’s mother — the identical parents named in Mauro’s 1910 birth registration. Primitivo is Mauro’s sibling: the 1930 Mexico National Census places both Mauro Cruz and Primitivo Cruz in the same household in Nueva Italia, Michoacán, alongside Miguel Cruz, Liborio Cruz, and Facundo Cruz — all single males born in Guerrero, consistent with confirmed civil registration birth places for those siblings. **Source:** original (civil marriage register). **Information:** secondary (the 1935 informants were the marriage parties or witnesses, not the parents themselves). **Evidence:** indirect for Mauro’s parentage (names Primitivo’s parents, not Mauro’s directly). Despite its indirect and secondary nature, this record independently identifies the same two parents and carries no familial relationship to the 1910 birth registration, making it a genuinely corroborating piece.

A naming discrepancy requires acknowledgment: Primitivo appears as “Cruz” in the 1930 census but as “Corona” in the 1935 marriage act. This reflects a Mexican naming pattern in which children informally registered or born outside a civil marriage sometimes used the mother’s paternal surname. The identity of Primitivo Cruz (1930) with Primitivo Corona (1935) is probable: same age group, same Guerrero origin, same co-residence with the four Cruz brothers and Apolinar Corona. This discrepancy does not impair the corroborating value of the 1935 marriage for identifying the parents.

### Corroborating Evidence: 1930 National Census

The 1930 Mexico National Census, Feliciano Anguiano household, Nueva Italia de Ruiz, Michoacán (FamilySearch, ark:/61903/3:1:S3HT-66R3-LH4) records Apolinar Corona — widowed, born Guerrero circa 1881 — residing with Mauro Cruz and his four Cruz brothers. **Source:** derivative (census schedule from a national enumeration). **Information:** secondary (census enumerators recorded what household members reported). **Evidence:** indirect for parentage — a widowed woman sharing the surname element “Corona” with the children’s maternal compound surname, cohabiting with all Cruz siblings, is circumstantially indicative of the mother–children relationship. Consistent with both the 1910 birth registration and the 1935 marriage.

### Discrepancy: Birth Year in the 1930 Census

The 1930 census records Mauro’s birth year as 1914, a four-year discrepancy from the civil registration’s 22 November 1910. Census ages in this era and region were routinely approximated. Comparison of siblings’ census birth years to their civil registration dates reveals a systematic underreporting of 2–4 years across the family (Miguel: civil 1913, census 1916; Liborio: civil 1916, census 1918; Facundo: civil 1918, census 1920). The civil birth registration, an original record with primary information filed 14 days after birth, governs. The census figure for birth year is derivative and secondary and carries no weight against a contemporaneous primary source.

## Proof Statement

The evidence conclusively establishes that Mauro Cruz Corona was born **22 November 1910** at **La Unión de Isidoro Montes de Oca, Guerrero, Mexico**, the son of **Sóstenes Cruz** (father) and **Apolinar Corona** (mother). Three independent records — the 1910 civil birth registration (direct, primary), the 1935 Primitivo Corona civil marriage (indirect, secondary, independently created), and the 1930 Mexico National Census (indirect, secondary) — consistently name the same two parents and are consistent with the birth date and place from the civil registration. No conflicting evidence undermines this conclusion. The conclusion remains open to revision if previously unknown evidence emerges.
